title: Dice Throne Adventures - A great game that can be hard to recommend
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yrJ1mvA6eIM

Daniel (@dungeondive) digs into Dice Throne Adventures, which he likens to the video game Puzzle Quest: a thematic-Yahtzee combat engine dressed up as an adventure game. You take an elaborate character (he's playing the Monk, naturally), roam node-to-node across modular dungeon tiles, and when an enemy spawns you fight a quick back-and-forth dice duel — matching straights, full houses and combos to trigger powers, manipulating rolls with a card deck. Defeat a level-4 minion, then a boss; repeat four times for a campaign.

The loot loop is the standout: treasure is found unidentified, then identified for gold between adventures and added to a deck that persists and grows even as each scenario resets your character to square one — satisfying light deck-building and quick re-leveling. It's gorgeous, colourful, superbly organized (one of the best chit trays he's seen), fast to set up, and genuinely "effortless fun." The catch — and why it's hard to recommend — is the price and barrier: it's not standalone, it's an *expansion* to Dice Throne, so you also need heroes, pushing the cost toward $120–150. A no-brainer if you already own Dice Throne; a hefty ask if you don't, for what remains a fairly small experience.

----

Dice Throne Adventures is, at heart, dressed-up Yahtzee — Daniel loves it but calls it a hard sell given the price and that it needs the base game. Does a steep barrier to entry kill your interest, even in a game you'd enjoy?
